coredata.dc:title: Functionalization of h-BN by the exfoliation and modification of carbon dots for enhancing corrosion resistance of waterborne epoxy coating
coredata.dc:creator: Wan, Shan
Chen, Huikai
Cai, Guangyi
Liao, Bokai
Guo, Xingpeng
coredata.dc:description: The amino-functionalized carbon dots (FCDs) were used as intercalators to exfoliate and modify the boron nitride for obtaining the FCDs/boron nitride nanosheets (BNNS) nanocomposite. The FCDs, BNNS and FCDs/BNNS were respectively dispersed into the waterborne epoxy (WEP) matrix to prepare the corresponding modified WEP coatings on the copper substrate through the spray coating method. Their micro/macro-morphologies, compositions, surface roughness, self-healing and corrosion resistance behaviors were comparatively researched. Electrochemical results and surface morphology observation show that WEP FCDs/BNNS coating possesses the optimal corrosion protection performance after 40 d immersion in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. And the impedance value (4.97 × 105 Ω cm2) of WEP FCDs/BNNS coating is still about an order of magnitude higher than that of pure WEP coating (3.36 × 104 Ω cm2). In the WEP FCDs/BNNS coating system, BNNS as physical barrier extend the diffusion path of corrosive species and block inherent defects of the coating, thus slowing down the corrosion rate; FCDs as intercalators strip BNNS enhancing its physical barrier effect. Besides, FCDs can also act as the effective corrosion inhibitors, which could adsorb on the exposed or corroded copper surface and hinder the further corrosion. Their synergistic effects of physical barrier performance from BNNS and self-healing capability from FCDs endow the WEP composite coating with outstanding corrosion resistance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Layered-structures
Nanocomposites
Polymer-matrix composites
Corrosion

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of BNNS, FCDs, FCDs/BNNS composite
Fabrication of WEP BNNS, WEP NZ, WEP NZ/BNNS composite coatings

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic diagram of preparation process of WEP FCDs/BNNS coating.
The SEM micro-morphology of boron nitride (a) and FCDs/BNNS (b); TEM images of FCDs/BNNS (c) and FCDs (d); FTIR (e) and XRD (f) spectra of BNNS, FCDs and FCDs/BNNS.
The SEM micro-morphology, the enlarged view and surface roughness of WEP (a1–a3), WEP FCDs (b1–b3) and WEP FCDs/BNNS (c1–c3) coatings.
EIS plots of WEP (a1–a3), WEP BNNS (b1–b3), WEP FCDs (c1–c3) and WEP FCDs/BNNS (d1–d3) coatings under different immersion time in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution.

The equivalent circuits (a), (b) and (c) for fitting the EIS plots of WEP composite coating in the immersion period; EIS diagrams (d), R
c , R
f , R
ct values (e) and CPE
c , CPE
f , CPE
dl values (f) for WEP BNNS coating after 40 d immersion under different bias.

The fitting parameters of R
c (a), R
ct (b) and R
t (c) for all WEP composite coatings in the immersion period.
The LEIS diagrams of WEP (a), WEP BNNS (b), WEP FCDs (c) and WEP FCDs/BNNS (d) coatings after 40 days immersion in the 3.5 wt% NaCl solution.
The optical morphologies of WEP (a), WEP BNNS (b), WEP FCDs (c) and WEP FCDs/BNNS (d) coatings after 40 days immersion in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution.
The corrosion protection mechanism of WEP (a), WEP BNNS (b), WEP FCDs (c) and WEP FCDs/BNNS (d) coatings immersing in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution.
The fitting parameters of all the WEP composite coatings in Fig. 4.

head.ce:title: Functionalization of h-BN by the exfoliation and modification of carbon dots for enhancing corrosion resistance of waterborne epoxy coating

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Joint Institute of Guangzhou University & Institute of Corrosion Science and Technology, Guangzhou University, Guangzhou 510006, China

Guangzhou Key Laboratory for Clean Energy and Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Key Laboratory of Science and Technology on Vessel Integrated Power System, Naval University of Engineering, Wuhan 430033, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Key Laboratory of Science and Technology on Vessel Integrated Power System

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Joint Institute of Guangzhou University & Institute of Corrosion Science and Technology, Guangzhou University, Guangzhou 510006, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Joint Institute of Guangzhou University & Institute of Corrosion Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The amino-functionalized carbon dots (FCDs) were used as intercalators to exfoliate and modify the boron nitride for obtaining the FCDs/boron nitride nanosheets (BNNS) nanocomposite. The FCDs, BNNS and FCDs/BNNS were respectively dispersed into the waterborne epoxy (WEP) matrix to prepare the corresponding modified WEP coatings on the copper substrate through the spray coating method. Their micro/macro-morphologies, compositions, surface roughness, self-healing and corrosion resistance behaviors were comparatively researched. Electrochemical results and surface morphology observation show that WEP FCDs/BNNS coating possesses the optimal corrosion protection performance after 40 d immersion in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. And the impedance value (4.97 × 105 Ω cm2) of WEP FCDs/BNNS coating is still about an order of magnitude higher than that of pure WEP coating (3.36 × 104 Ω cm2). In the WEP FCDs/BNNS coating system, BNNS as physical barrier extend the diffusion path of corrosive species and block inherent defects of the coating, thus slowing down the corrosion rate; FCDs as intercalators strip BNNS enhancing its physical barrier effect. Besides, FCDs can also act as the effective corrosion inhibitors, which could adsorb on the exposed or corroded copper surface and hinder the further corrosion. Their synergistic effects of physical barrier performance from BNNS and self-healing capability from FCDs endow the WEP composite coating with outstanding corrosion resistance.

ce:section.ce:para: Water-borne epoxy coating (WEP) [1–3] has been widely applied in the field of corrosion protection, due to the low content of volatile compounds, strong adhesion on metals, electrical insulation, as well as favourable mechanical property. Although WEP has been commercialized and highly appreciated, it couldn't provide the sufficient long-term protection for metal corrosion owing to the formation of micro-pore and micro-crack in the curing process [4–6]. Furthermore, the hydrophilic groups or compounds in the WEP are susceptible to the penetration of corrosive medium, such as oxygen or water molecules, thus accelerating the corrosion process. Therefore, numerous methods have been attempted to enhance the anticorrosion performance of WEP [7–9]. Among them, the incorporation of nanofillers in the WEP coating is regarded as an effective method for improving the long-term anticorrosion capability.

The hexagonal boron nitride nanosheets (BNNS) [10–12], as graphene-like two-dimensional material, have been applied as nanofillers for enhancing the anticorrosion capacity of WEP. On the one hand, BNNS own excellent chemical inertness and impermeability, which can act as the physical barrier for preventing the infiltration of corrosive medium, including water molecules, chloride ions, etc.; on the other hand, BNNS are electrically insulated and won't cause the galvanic corrosion like graphene. However, the strong lip-lip interactions of adjacent BNNS result in the aggregation owing to the large electronegativity difference between N and B atoms, which can inversely damage the integrity of coating. Many strategies based on chemical modification or/and non-covalent functionalization have been reported to exfoliate BNNS. Shi et al. [13] reported the anticorrosion reinforcement of WEP by polyethylenimine-assisting exfoliated BNNS. Wu et al. [14] applied the methods of mechanical exfoliation and chemical modification to prepare BNNS, which could significantly improve the protection performance of WEP. Lu et al. [15] functionalized BNNS through in situ polymerization of pyrrole, and this incorporation into the epoxy matrix leads to the excellent anti-corrosion resistance. BNNS can be used as the physical barrier in the epoxy matrix, and enhance the corrosion resistance of the composite coating through extending the diffusion path of corrosive species. However, the addition of BNNS couldn't provide the active protection and self-healing capability for coatings. The protection performance of WEP coating is limited only by adding BNNS, especially for the locally damaged coating.

Recently, carbon dots [16–19] as one kind of carbon nanomaterials have been focused due to the adjustable fluorescence emissions, facile production and modification, high aqueous solubility and favourable biocompatibility. Zhe et al. [20] reported carbon dots as fillers could induce healing/self-healing and anti-corrosion properties in the polymer coating. Ramezanzadeh et al. [21] improved corrosion protection performance of the epoxy coating by incorporation of polyaniline tailored graphene oxide quantum dot. Ye et al. [22] applied carbon dot-intercalated graphene for enhancing the self-healing and corrosion resistance of WEP coating. Carbon dots are easily modified and possess various functional groups [23–25]. On one hand, amino-functionalized carbon dots (FCDs) can be taken as intercalators for exfoliating boron nitride, through the complexation between Lewis bases from FCDs and electron-deficient boron atoms; on the other hand, FCDs can also be used as effective corrosion inhibitors to endow the FCDs/BNNS nanocomposite with anti-corrosion capability.

Herein, the FCDs/BNNS nanocomposite was firstly used for enhancing corrosion resistance of waterborne epoxy coating. The compatibility and dispersibility of BNNS in the WEP matrix are significantly improved using FCDs. Moreover, FCDs also endow the WEP composite coating with a certain self-healing ability. The WEP FCDs/BNNS coating was comparatively researched with WEP, WEP FCDs and WEP BNNS coatings through electrochemical tests (electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS), local electrochemical impedance spectra (LEIS)) and surface analysis (Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray diffractometer (XRD), transmission electron microscope (TEM), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), 3D optical microscope and surface roughness analysis). The synergistic effect of physical barrier and self-healing ability for FCDs/BNNS in the WEP coating is systematically studied, thus revealing protection mechanism of the composite coating.

Glucose, ascorbic acid, sodium chloride, absolute ethyl alcohol, N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), sodium hydroxide, and acetone were provided by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Corporation. 4-Amino-3-hydrazine-5-mercapto-1,2,4-triazole was purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation. The distilled water was used for experiments with the resistivity of 18 MΩ cm. The waterborne epoxy resin (D-1900) and curing agent (D-1900B) was purchased from Guangdong Deliya New Materials Co. Ltd. The original source, chemical structure of epoxy polymer is condensation polymer of epichlorohydrin, bisphenol A, and polyoxyethylene.

Copper electrodes (99.99%) with size of 10 mm length × 10 mm width × 3 mm depth were purchased and processed by Shandong Yangxin Technology Co. Ltd. The machined electrodes were fixed on the experimental plastic board and surrounded by the polytetrafluoroethylene tubes. Then epoxy resin was used to fill with leaving an exposed electrode area of 1 cm2 contacting the corrosive electrolyte. After the curing of epoxy resin, the abrasive papers with different mesh (400, 800 and 1200 grade) were applied to remove exposed corrosion products of copper surface. The polished copper electrodes were successively cleaned with acetone, absolute ethyl alcohol and deionized water in an ultrasonic cleaner, and dried by cold air. Finally, the pre-treated electrodes were stored in the desiccator for later tests.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of BNNS, FCDs, FCDs/BNNS composite
ce:section.ce:para: The 0.2 g boron nitride was dissolved in the 180 mL DMF, sonicated at 50 °C for 4 h and centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 30 min, in order to remove the aggregated and unexfoliated boron nitride. The remaining supernatant liquid was filtrated to obtain the objective products (BNNS) on the filter cake, and then dried at 60 °C for 24 h in a blast air oven.

The detailed synthesis process of FCDs were described according to our previous work [23]. The 0.2 g boron nitride and 0.02 g FCDs was dissolved in the 90 mL DMF and 90 mL water. The mixture was conducted with the same procedures of BNNS preparation, for acquiring the FCDs/BNNS composite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of WEP BNNS, WEP NZ, WEP NZ/BNNS composite coatings
ce:section.ce:para: The schematic diagram of preparation process of WEP FCDs/BNNS coating on the copper electrode is shown in Fig. 1 . The preparation procedures of WEP BNNS, WEP FCDs, and WEP FCDs/BNNS coatings are as follows: firstly, 0.05 g BNNS, FCDs, and FCDs/BNNS nanocomposites were ultrasonically dissolved in 10 mL ethanol for 0.5 h; 10 g epoxy resin was poured into the above mixture with ceaselessly stirring for 0.5 h; then 6 mL curing agent was added and stirred homogeneously; the suspension was finally sprayed on the copper electrodes with a spray gun (W71, Mingzhi, Japan). The obtained wet WEP, WEP FCDs, WEP BNNS and WEP FCDs/BNNS coatings were cured at 50 °C for 12 h in a blast air oven with an average dry thickness of 50 ± 5 μm.

The chemical composition, structure and micro-morphology of h-BN, BNNS, FCDs, FCDs/BNNS was characterized by FTIR (Bruker, Tensor II + Hyperion 2000, Germany), XRD (XD-3, Beijing Purkinje General Instrument Co., Ltd.), TEM (JEOL JEM-2100F, Japan) and SEM (JEOL, JSM-7001F, Japan). The corrosion morphologies of WEP, WEP FCDs, WEP BNNS and WEP FCDs/BNNS coatings were observed by an optical microscope (Betical XTL-6745 J4, China) after 40 days immersion in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. A magnetic-sense coating thickness gauge (GM 998, Guangzhou LX, China) was adopted to measure the thickness of all WEP coating.

The EIS diagrams of WEP, WEP BNNS, WEP FCDs, WEP FCDs/BNNS coatings were conducted in 3.5 wt% NaCl solutions through an electrochemical workstation (CS350H, Corrtest, China) with a three-electrode system. The coated copper electrodes were taken as work electrodes, and the platinum conductance electrode and saturated calomel electrode were counter electrode and reference electrode, respectively. The frequency range of EIS is 100 kHz to 10 mHz with a 10 mV sine wave disturbance and 10 logarithmic steps per decade. The ZView2® software was used to analyse the EIS results. The LEIS test was carried out by the VersaSCAN operating system (VersaSCAN, AMETEK, American) to research the corrosion degree of all WEP coatings after 40 days immersion in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. The LEIS technology applied two micro platinum electrodes to measure the local impedance with a 20 mV of sinusoidal disturbance voltage by measuring the alternating current density of local corrosion medium on the metal surface. The scanning area of LEIS is about 6 mm × 6 mm with recording the impedance values at 1 kHz.

Fig. 2(a)–(b) shows the SEM micro-morphologies of boron nitride and FCDs/BNNS. The boron nitride in Fig. 2(a) shows a flower-like and lamellar stacking structure. After exfoliation in the aqueous solution containing FCDs inhibitor, the FCDs/BNNS (Fig. 2(b)) presents laminated structure and the interlamellar spacing increases, and the corresponding TEM image is shown in Fig. 2(c). The TEM image of FCDs in Fig. 2(d) displays circular structure with a diameter of 5– 7 nm. Fig. 2(e)–(f) shows the FTIR and XRD spectra of BNNS, FCDs and FCDs/BNNS. From Fig. 2(e), the adsorption peaks at 796 and 1386 cm−1 appear in the BNNS and FCDs/BNNS spectra, which are assigned to BNB stretching vibrations and in plane BN transverse optional mode [15], respectively. The characteristic peaks at 796 and 1386 cm−1 for FCDs/BNNS spectra are related to OH and CH bonds in the alkyl chain from FCDs. The characteristic diffraction peaks at 26.6°, 41.7°, 54.9° and 76° in the XRD spectra (Fig. 2(f)) correspond to the planes of the hexagonal crystal of BN. Moreover, the appearance of a series of diffraction peaks at 31.71°, 45.49°, 66.36° and 75.11°, could be ascribed to the characteristic carbon crystal pattern according to the standard PDF card 00-046-0943 [26,27].

Fig. 3 shows the SEM micro-morphology, the enlarged view and surface roughness of WEP, WEP FCDs and WEP FCDs/BNNS coatings. As for WEP coating in Fig. 3(a), it presents a fairly smooth structure and contains a large amount of tiny particles with the surface roughness of 12.87 μm. After the addition of FCDs, the obvious aggregation phenomenon appears in the WEP CDs coating (Fig. 3(b)) and surface roughness increases to 14.99 μm. From the WEP CDs/BNNS coating in Fig. 3(c), it is composed of crosslinked particles with different size. The incorporation of CDs/BNNS composite in the WEP coating makes the surface roughness decrease to 9.38 μm, in comparison with the pure WEP coating.

To research the long-term anticorrosion performance of all the above WEP composite coatings, saline immersion experiments were adopted as an accelerated durability test. Electrochemical impedance spectra [28,29] were carried out to deeply investigate the surface/interface reactions between the coatings and copper substrate. And it can be used to real-time monitor the long-term corrosion resistance for WEP, WEP BNNS, WEP FCDs and WEP FCDs/BNNS coatings. Fig. 4 shows the EIS plots of all WEP composite coatings after different immersion time in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. Generally, the larger diameter of capacitance arc in the Nyquist diagram usually reflects better corrosion resistance. Obviously, the capacitance arc diameter of WEP coating in Fig. 4(a) is the smallest among all the WEP composite coatings, suggesting its poor anti-corrosion performance. Moreover, all the EIS plots in Fig. 4(a) for WEP coating show two time constants, which could be attributed to the coating at high frequency and the charge transfer process at low frequency. It could be fitted by the equivalent circuit in Fig. 5(a). Among them, R
s , R
ct , R
c , CPE
dl and CPE
c represent solution resistance, charge transfer resistance, coating resistance, constant phase angle element of double electric layer and the coating, respectively. With the extend of immersion time, the impedance value for WEP coating gradually decreases, suggesting gradually diminished anti-corrosion capability.

After the introduction of BNNS in the WEP coating in Fig. 4(b), the impedance value increases to 2.67 × 105 Ω cm2 compared with 4.39 × 104 Ω cm2 of pure WEP coating. It can be mainly ascribed to that BNNS as physical barrier can extend the diffusion path of corrosive species and thus enhance the physical blocking performance of the composite coating. Similarly, the EIS plots for WEP BNNS coating present two capacitive reactance arcs in the initial 20-days immersion, which could be fitted by equivalent circuit (a). While in the later immersion, three time constants appear in the EIS diagrams, and the additional time constant could be ascribed to the resistance and capacitance of corrosion product films. However, it is hard to clearly judge whether equivalent circuit Fig. 5(b) or Fig. 5(c) is suitable to fit the above EIS data.

To verify the accuracy of the chosen equivalent circuit model, the EIS tests of WEP BNNS coating after 40 d immersion were carried out under the small applied bias condition (0, 10, and 20 mV). The corresponding EIS results are shown in Fig. 5(d) and it is found that the capacitance arc diameter at low frequency obviously decreases with an increase of applied bias. It is known that the disturbance of bias voltage mainly influences the charge transfer process significantly. So it can be judged that the capacitance arc at low frequency is related to charge transfer resistance and double electrical layer capacitance, while the capacitance arcs at medium and high frequency are attributed to the resistance and capacitance of corrosion product film and the coating, respectively. Therefore, the equivalent circuit Fig. 5(b) can be suitable to fit the above EIS plots. The detailed fitting results are presented in Fig. 5(e) and (f). Obviously, R
ct decreases while CPE
dl increases dramatically with the elevating bias, while the other fitted parameters (R
c , R
f , CPE
c , CPE
f ) change slightly. Thus, these results exactly verify the correctness of equivalent circuit in Fig. 5(b) for three time constants appearing in the EIS diagrams.

After individual incorporation of FCDs in the WEP coating, their EIS plots in Fig. 4(c) show two time constants in the preliminary 10-days immersion stage and three time constants in the later immersion stage, which could be fitted by equivalent circuit Fig. 5(a) and (b) respectively. The impedance value (9.99 × 104 Ω cm2) of WEP FCDs coating increases compared to pure WEP coating (4.39 × 104 Ω cm2), indicating that the introduction of FCDs in the WEP coating improves its anti-corrosion performance. Moreover, the impedance value for WEP FCDs coating firstly decreases and then increases when immersing in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution in the medium period. It could be ascribed to that in the initial stage, the diffusion of corrosive medium into the coating induce corrosion, resulting in the decrease of protectiveness of WEP FCDs coating; while in the medium immersion period, the FCDs in the coating will diffuse to the corroded area, adsorb on the metal surface and form a protective film, thus impeding further occurrence of corrosion. Owing to the insufficient protection performance of FCDs adsorption film, the impedance value for WEP FCDs coating decreases in the late immersion period.

In Fig. 4(d), the WEP FCDs/BNNS coating reaches the maximum impedance value and exhibits the optimal anticorrosion effect among all of the above coatings. In the whole immersion period, the impedance value for WEP FCDs/BNNS coating is much higher than those of WEP, WEP BNNS and WEP FCDs coatings. With the prolonging of immersion time, although the impedance value for WEP FCDs/BNNS coating decreases slightly, it is still superior to those for other WEP composite coatings. The outstanding corrosion protection performance for WEP FCDs/BNNS coating is derived from the synergistic effects of FCDs and BNNS in the WEP composite coating. In the whole immersion period, the EIS plots for WEP FCDs/BNNS coating in Fig. 4(d) always exhibit two characteristic capacitance arcs. The equivalent circuit Fig. 5(a) could be used to fit them. In comparison with WEP FCDs and WEP BNNS coatings, the additional time constant corresponding to corrosion product film for WEP FCDs/BNNS coating doesn't appear in the whole immersion, suggesting that there is no or little corrosion products between the coating and the copper substrate (Table 1 ). It implies excellent corrosion resistance of the WEP FCDs/BNNS coating.

To further analyse the variation tendencies of all the WEP composite coatings during the long-term immersion test, the fitting parameters of R
c , R
ct and R
t are summarized in Fig. 6 . From Fig. 6(a), the R
c for WEP coating decreases in the later immersion period, while for WEP FCDs or WEP BNNS coating, it shows a rising trend in the initial stage and keeps invariable in the later stage, higher than that for pure WEP coating, suggesting that individual incorporation of BNNS or FCDs in the WEP coating improves the corrosion protectiveness of coating. When the FCDs/BNNS is introduced in the WEP coating, the R
c significantly increases compared with other coating systems. And it decreases after 10 days immersion and then stays stable, which is always 1– 2 order of magnitudes higher than that of pure WEP coating. It implies that the FCDs/BNNS composite dramatically enhances the anti-corrosion property of the WEP composite coating. Fig. 6(b) exhibits the corresponding charge transfer resistance (R
ct ). For WEP and WEP BNNS coatings, R
ct values decrease with the prolonging of immersion time, and the R
ct of WEP BNNS coating is higher than that of WEP coating, indicating that although the introduction of BNNS into WEP coating extends the diffusion path of corrosive medium and slows down the corrosion process, it cannot provide self-healing ability. After the incorporation of FCDs into WEP coating, the R
ct firstly decreases and then increases in the prometaphase of immersion. The penetration of corrosive ions across the coating on the metal surface initially induces the occurrence of corrosion, and then FCDs inhibitor diffuses into the corroded area and forms a protective adsorption film, thus inhibiting further corrosion of the metal substrate. It demonstrates the self-healing capacity of the WEP FCDs coating. While, in the later immersion stage, the R
ct for WEP FCDs coating decreases, mainly attributing to inadequate protectiveness of FCDs adsorption film in the coating. In comparison, the R
ct for WEP FCDs/BNNS coating firstly decreases in the preliminary stage and only increases in the later stage gradually, much higher than that for other WEP composite coatings. It suggests that the charge transfer process under WEP FCDs/BNNS coating on copper substrate is the slowest and the copper corrosion is dramatically retarded, which could be ascribed to the physical barrier property of BNNS and self-healing capacity of FCDs from WEP FCDs/BNNS coating.

The total resistance (R
t ) is the sum of R
c , R
ct and R
f if existing, regarded as a parameter for evaluating long-term corrosion resistance for all the WEP composite coatings in saline immersion period [30,31]. For WEP coating, the impedance value gradually decreases, indicating that its anti-corrosion performance became weak in the soaking process. After the introduction of BNNS in the WEP coating, its impedance value obviously increases because BNNS as a barrier in the coating forms the labyrinth effect, and thus prolonging the penetration way of corrosive medium and retarding the corrosion process. While the R
t for WEP BNNS coating decreases with extend of immersion time, suggesting that individual effect from physical barriers of BNNS is limited, and it doesn't possess self-healing capability. With the incorporation of FCDs in the WEP coating, the R
t only slightly improves compared to that of WEP coating. With the prolonging of immersion time, it firstly decreases, then increases and finally decreases. It could be accounted for as follows: in the initial stage, the ingress of corrosion medium induces corrosion of copper; in the middle stage, the formation of adsorption film of FCDs on the exposed or corroded area blocks further corrosion; in the later stage, the corrosion protection from FCDs adsorption film is insufficient, leading to the decrease of corrosion resistance in the final stage. After adding FCDs/BNNS in the WEP coating, its R
t is much higher than that of WEP, WEP FCDs and WEP BNNS coatings in the whole immersion period, indicating that WEP FCDs/BNNS coating exhibits the optimal corrosion protection performance. Meanwhile, the R
t for WEP FCDs/BNNS coating presents a tendency of firstly decreasing and then increasing. The prior decrease of R
t is ascribed to that water permeation across the coating on the copper surface causes corrosion. The later increase of R
t lies to the synergistic effect of physical barrier from BNNS and a certain self-healing ability from adsorption and protective film formation of FCDs on the copper substrate, which endows the WEP FCDs/BNNS coating excellent anti-corrosion capability.

To further analyse the local corrosion situation [32,33] for all the above coatings after 40 days immersion in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution, LEIS technique was carried out and corresponding results were shown in Fig. 7 . Obviously, the impedance value for WEP coating is the lowest, in comparison with WEP FCDs, WEP BNNS and WEP FCDs/BNNS coatings. It suggests that the protective performance of WEP coating is the worst. After the incorporation of FCDs, BNNS or FCDs/BNNS into the WEP coatings, the impedance value increases by about an order of magnitude, implying that the introduction of nanocomposite all improves the corrosion protection ability of the WEP composite coating. Among them, the average surface-distribution impedance values for WEP FCDs/BNNS coating are higher than those for WEP FCDs and BNNS coating. It signifies that after 40 days immersion, the overall corrosion protection efficiency is greatest for WEP FCDs/BNNS coating. It is consistent with the EIS consequences.

The surface morphology of all the WEP composite coatings after 40 days immersion in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution was also observed by optical microscope, as shown in Fig. 8 . From Fig. 8(a), abundant corrosion products appear on the surface of WEP coating, suggesting the occurrence of severe copper corrosion under the coating. With the BNNS incorporation into the WEP coating in Fig. 8(b), the corrosion degree alleviates. Only few corrosion products and water penetration phenomenon are observed on the WEP BNNS coating, implying that BNNS as physical barrier improve the corrosion protection efficiency to some extent. It is seen from Fig. 8(c) that there are lots of black spots and several blisters for WEP CDs coating after immersion, possibly attributing to that the ingress of corrosive ions across the coating induces corrosion, and adsorption film formation of FCDs holds back further corrosion on the copper substrate. After the introduction of FCDs/BNNS into the WEP coating, the surface of WEP FCDs/BNNS coating in Fig. 8(d) is nearly invariable after 40 days immersion, illustrating that the FCDs/BNNS significantly improves the corrosion protection performance of WEP composite coating. It is in accordance with the above electrochemical results.

Fig. 9 shows the schematic diagram of anticorrosion mechanism for all the WEP composite coatings, based on the electrochemical results and surface morphology analysis. For pure WEP coating in Fig. 9(a), corrosive medium (oxygen, water, chloride ions, etc.) easily penetrates across the coating into the copper substrate and causing the serious corrosion [34], owing to the existence of large amounts of inherent defects (micropores, etc.). With the introduction of BNNS in the WEP coating (Fig. 9(b)), BNNS are regarded as physical barrier and evenly distributed into the coating, which can form the labyrinth effect and extend the diffusion path of corrosive medium [15,35], thus retarding the corrosion process. Furthermore, BNNS could be used for blocking defects of the coating, enhancing physical barrier property of the WEP composite coating. However, the enhanced corrosion protection performance by individual addition of BNNS is insufficient, especially when the penetration of corrosive species across the coating causes corrosion of copper substrate. In addition, the BNNS is apt to agglomerate in the WEP coating owing to the high specific surface area and interlayer force, which may destroy the integrity of WEP coating. In Fig. 9(c), when FCDs are solely added into the WEP coating, although FCDs possess certain corrosion inhibition effect on copper surface [22], the inhibition performance is inadequate owing to that only small amount of FCDs in the coating release and hinder further corrosion of copper. In comparison with individual FCDs or BNNS incorporation into the WEP coating, the FCDs/BNNS composite decreases the aggregation and improves the dispersity in the WEP coating (Fig. 9(d)). Because FCDs intercalate between BNNS, which significantly weakens their interlayer force [36]. On one hand, BNNS as physical barrier not only fill the inherent defects of WEP coating, but also uniformly disperse in the coating through the intercalation and modification of FCDs. It can form the labyrinth effect and prolong the diffusion path of corrosive medium, which thus endows the WEP FCDs/BNNS coating excellent barrier property. On the other hand, the FCDs could adsorb on the corroded or exposed copper surface through physical adsorption and chemical coordination bond between FCDs and copper substrate, and form a protection film, which effectively holds back further corrosion [37]. The above two-sided effects, mainly containing the excellent physical barrier performance from BNNS and the certain self-healing performance from FCDs, synergistically enhance the long-term corrosion resistance of WEP composite coating.

In this work, boron nitride was exfoliated modified by FCDs to obtain the FCDs/BNNS composite, and its micro-structure and composition was characterized by SEM, TEM, FTIR and XRD techniques. The FCDs, BNNS and FCDs/BNNS composite were incorporated into the WEP coating on the copper substrate through a spray coating method. The results of EIS, LEIS and morphology observations all show that WEP FCDs/BNNS coating exhibits the optimum corrosion protection performance among the four WEP composite coatings. Individual introduction of FCDs or BNNS only improves protection performance of the WEP composite coating slightly, while the FCDs/BNNS composite significantly enhances the corrosion resistance of the WEP composite coating, in comparison with pure WEP coating. On one side, the FCDs/BNNS composite strengthens the dispersity and homogeneity of BNNS into the WEP coating by intercalating FCDs and lowering the interlayer force; on the other side, it forms the labyrinth effect through the prolong of diffusion path of corrosion medium and the block of inherent defects in the coating. Thus, the physical barrier property for WEP FCDs/BNNS coating is dramatically enhanced. Moreover, FCDs as effective corrosion inhibitor in the coating can absorb on the exposed or corroded copper surface and form a protective film, thus providing a certain self-healing capacity. Therefore, the FCDs/BNNS composite synergistically enhances long-term corrosion resistance of the WEP coating.

Shan Wan: Investigation, Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Huikai Chen: Investigation, Formal analysis. Guangyi Cai: Formal analysis, Visualization. Bokai Liao: Writing – review & editing. Xingpeng Guo: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors Shan Wan and Huikai Chen contributed equally to this work. The authors want to thank the following foundations for the financial supports to this work: The National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 51971067, 52001080) and The China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (Grant No. 2020M682650).
